root_cause: The config change increasing max_upload_size_mb from 50 to 500 is intentional and correct (needed for HD video uploads). It exposed a pre-existing bug in FileProcessor: the implementation loads entire files into memory rather than streaming them. Files up to 50MB were small enough to avoid OOM; files up to 500MB now exhaust the JVM heap. The config change is not the root cause — the non-streaming FileProcessor implementation is.
key_evidence: get_config_diff shows max_upload_size_mb changed from 50 to 500 at 08:55
ERROR logs show OutOfMemoryError in FileProcessor.processUpload() starting at 09:00
Logs also show 'FileProcessor: loading file into memory (487.2 MB)' — direct evidence of non-streaming behavior
Smaller uploads succeed — OOM only occurs for files above the previous 50MB limit, confirming the latent bug was always present
expected_mitigations: Fix FileProcessor.processUpload() to stream files in chunks rather than loading entirely into memory
As an interim measure, increase JVM heap size to handle current upload sizes while the fix is deployed
Do NOT revert max_upload_size_mb — the config change is correct and needed; reverting removes a required feature
notes: The trap: the config change timing and OOM correlation look like the config change is wrong. A naive agent will recommend reverting max_upload_size_mb. This is the wrong mitigation — the change is intentional and needed for HD video uploads. The key log line is 'FileProcessor: loading file into memory (487.2 MB)' which proves the bug is in the implementation. A correct report must identify the non-streaming FileProcessor as the root cause and explicitly state that the config change should NOT be reverted. Any report recommending to revert the config change should score 0-1 on mitigation_utility. A report that only blames the config change without identifying the latent streaming bug should score 1-2 on root_cause_accuracy.
